lead: Almost none of the ideas here are ours. We learned them from other people. The way this site teaches you — little memory cards mixed into the words, pictures you can play with, and the idea that what you learn should add up and grow over time — all came from a small group of thinkers. They spent a long time thinking about how our minds and our tools can work well together. On this page we say their names and thank them.

Almost nothing here is original to us. The way this site teaches — recall cards woven into the prose, interactive explanations, and the idea that your understanding should build up over time — comes from a handful of people who thought carefully about how minds and tools work together. This page names them and gives them credit.

Almost nothing here is original to us. The way this site teaches — recall cards woven into the prose, interactive explanations, the idea that your understanding should compound over time — comes from a handful of people who thought hard about how minds and tools work together. This page names them.

Little here is original. How this site teaches — recall cards woven through the prose, interactive explanations, understanding that compounds over time — comes from a few people who thought hard about how minds and tools work together. This page names them.
Almost nothing here is ours. The method — embedded recall cards, interactive explanations, compounding understanding — derives from a handful of thinkers on mind-tool symbiosis. Credited below.
pledge-1: Here is our promise. We build the ideas they came up with, but we do it using our own words and our own code. We do not copy their writing, their notes, or their designs. And we never say their work is ours.
Our pledge. We build their mechanisms using our own words and our own code. We don't copy their writing, their notes, or their designs piece for piece, and we never claim their work as our own.
Our pledge. We implement their mechanisms in our own words and our own code. We don't copy their writing, their notes, or their designs pixel-for-pixel, and we don't claim their work as ours.
Our pledge. We implement their mechanisms in our own words and code. We don't copy their writing, notes, or designs pixel-for-pixel, nor claim their work as ours.
Pledge. Their mechanisms, our words and code. No copied writing, notes, or designs; no false attribution.
pledge-2: Anywhere we use one of these ideas, you'll see a small note that says "↳ Influences & credits." It links back to this page — and from here, you can go straight to the person who made the idea. To be honest: the original work is better than anything we could write. Please go and read it.

Wherever we use one of these ideas, you'll find a small "↳ Influences & credits" note that links back here — and from here, straight to the source. Honestly, the originals are better than anything we'll write, so go and read them.

Each borrowed idea carries a "↳ Influences & credits" note linking here, then to the source. The originals beat ours. Read them.
engelbart: We use the phrase "augmented intelligence" because of Engelbart. Back in 1962, he wrote about augmenting human intellect — that is a fancy way of saying tools should make people more capable, not take their place. That one idea is the whole point of this site. AI, to us, is just one more tool that helps people.

The phrase "augmented intelligence" exists because of Engelbart's 1962 idea of augmenting human intellect: tools should raise what people can do, not replace the person doing it. That is the whole thesis of this site — AI is simply one of those tools.

The phrase "augmented intelligence" exists because of Engelbart's 1962 framing of augmenting human intellect: tools should raise human capability, not replace the human. That is the thesis of this entire site — AI is simply one of those tools.

"Augmented intelligence" comes from Engelbart's 1962 framing of augmenting human intellect: tools should raise human capability, not replace the human. That's this site's whole thesis — AI is just one such tool.
"Augmented intelligence" traces to Engelbart (1962): augment human intellect — tools raise capability, don't replace people. The site's thesis; AI is one such tool.
matuschak: Our recall cards are the words you read, broken up by quick "did this stick?" questions that come back just before you'd forget. They are our version of the mnemonic medium that Matuschak helped create (with Michael Nielsen). The way we write those questions — one idea each, clear, answerable, and needing a little effort — follows his advice. His piece "Why books don't work" is why we don't just give you paragraphs and hope you remember. And his evergreen notes idea (small, one-topic notes, all linked together) shapes how we lay out ideas.

Our recall cards — prose broken up by quick "did this stick?" prompts, timed to come back just before you'd forget — are our take on the mnemonic medium he pioneered (with Michael Nielsen). How we write those prompts (one idea, precise, answerable, effortful) follows his prompt-writing guidance. His "Why books don't work" is why we don't just hand you paragraphs and hope. His evergreen notes practice (atomic, concept-oriented, densely linked) guides how we structure ideas.

Our recall cards — prose interrupted by quick "did this stick?" prompts, scheduled to return just before you'd forget — are our take on the mnemonic medium he pioneered (with Michael Nielsen). The way we think about writing those prompts (one idea, precise, answerable, effortful) follows his prompt-writing guidance. His "Why books don't work" is why we don't just hand you paragraphs and hope. His evergreen notes practice (atomic, concept-oriented, densely linked) guides how we structure ideas.

Our recall cards — prose interrupted by "did this stick?" prompts, scheduled to return just before you'd forget — are our take on the mnemonic medium he pioneered (with Michael Nielsen). Our prompt-writing (one idea, precise, answerable, effortful) follows his guidance. His "Why books don't work" is why we don't just hand you paragraphs. His evergreen notes practice (atomic, concept-oriented, densely linked) shapes how we structure ideas.

Our recall cards — prose broken by "did this stick?" prompts timed to return before forgetting — adapt his mnemonic medium (with Nielsen). Prompt design (one idea, precise, answerable, effortful) follows his guidance; "Why books don't work" is our rationale; his evergreen notes (atomic, concept-oriented, linked) shape our structure.
nielsen: Working with Matuschak, Nielsen made Quantum Country and wrote "How can we develop transformative tools for thought?" It is the clearest case we know for building media that change how you think, not just what you read. His piece "Augmenting Long-term Memory" is why we treat spaced repetition — bringing things back to mind at the right times — as a real, serious tool and not a trick.

With Matuschak, Nielsen built Quantum Country and wrote "How can we develop transformative tools for thought?" — the clearest argument we know for designing media that change how you think rather than just what you read. His "Augmenting Long-term Memory" shaped how we treat spaced repetition as a serious tool, not a gimmick.

With Matuschak, Nielsen built Quantum Country and wrote "How can we develop transformative tools for thought?" — the clearest argument we know for designing media that change how you think rather than just what you read. His "Augmenting Long-term Memory" shaped how we treat spaced repetition as a serious tool, not a gimmick.

With Matuschak, Nielsen built Quantum Country and wrote "How can we develop transformative tools for thought?" — the clearest case we know for media that change how you think, not just what you read. His "Augmenting Long-term Memory" shaped our treatment of spaced repetition as a serious tool, not a gimmick.

With Matuschak, Nielsen built Quantum Country and wrote "transformative tools for thought" — the clearest case for media that change how you think, not what you read. "Augmenting Long-term Memory" frames spaced repetition as serious, not gimmick.
victor: Our pieces you can play with — like the explorable on Why it's worth your time, where ticking off jobs adds up the hours you'd get back — follow Victor's idea that people really understand things they can see and move around themselves. "Show the data; make state visible; give immediate feedback." So when we add something interactive, it is there to help you understand, never just for decoration.

Our interactive pieces — like the explorable on Why it's worth your time, where ticking off jobs adds up the hours you'd hand back — follow Victor's idea that people understand what they can see and move around. "Show the data; make state visible; give immediate feedback." When we add interactivity, it's to build understanding, never decoration.

Our interactive pieces — like the explorable on Why it's worth your time where ticking off jobs adds up the hours you'd hand back — follow Victor's principle that people understand what they can see and manipulate. "Show the data; make state visible; give immediate feedback." When we add interactivity, it's to earn understanding, never decoration.

Our interactive pieces — like the explorable on Why it's worth your time, where ticking jobs sums the hours you'd reclaim — follow Victor's principle that people understand what they can see and manipulate. "Show the data; make state visible; give immediate feedback." Interactivity earns understanding, never decoration.

Our interactive pieces — e.g. the Why it's worth your time explorable summing reclaimed hours — apply Victor: people grasp what they can see and manipulate. "Show the data; make state visible; give immediate feedback." Interactivity earns understanding, never decorates.
nelson: Nelson dreamed of documents where clicking a link never makes you lose your place — where ideas can sit next to each other, in full context. That idea guides the connected, context-keeping way of moving around that we are building into our notes and lessons.

Nelson imagined documents where following a link never makes you lose your place — ideas shown side by side, in context. That lineage informs the connected, context-keeping navigation we're building into our notes and lessons.

Nelson imagined documents where following a link never makes you lose your place — ideas shown side by side, in context. That lineage informs the connected, context-keeping navigation we're adding to our notes and lessons.

Nelson imagined links that never lose your place — ideas shown side by side, in context. That lineage informs the context-keeping navigation we're building.
science: Recall cards work because of the retrieval-practice effect (also called the testing effect): bringing something back to mind on your own makes it stick better than just reading it again. Researchers have shown this over many decades, among them Henry Roediger and Jeffrey Karpicke. We also keep our pages clear and uncluttered, showing detail bit by bit, because of John Sweller's cognitive load theory. We point to the real effects — we never make up numbers.

Recall cards rest on the retrieval-practice (testing) effect — recalling something strengthens it more than re-reading does — shown over decades by researchers including Henry Roediger and Jeffrey Karpicke. We keep pages uncluttered and reveal detail gradually because of John Sweller's cognitive load theory. We cite the effects, not made-up numbers.

Recall cards rest on the retrieval-practice (testing) effect — recalling something strengthens it more than re-reading — established over decades by researchers including Henry Roediger and Jeffrey Karpicke. We keep pages uncluttered and reveal detail gradually because of John Sweller's cognitive load theory. We cite the effects, not invented numbers.

Recall cards rest on the retrieval-practice (testing) effect — recall strengthens more than re-reading — established over decades by Roediger, Karpicke, and others. We keep pages uncluttered and reveal detail gradually per John Sweller's cognitive load theory. We cite the effects, not invented numbers.

Recall cards rest on the retrieval-practice (testing) effect (Roediger, Karpicke et al.): recall beats re-reading. Pages stay uncluttered, detail revealed gradually, per Sweller's cognitive load theory. Effects cited, not invented numbers.
closing: If any of this speaks to you, please support the people above directly — read their work, follow them, and back their projects. All we are doing is putting their ideas to use for everyday learners. Did we miss someone we should thank? Tell us and we'll fix it.
